title: System Security Requirements and Security Levels
verificationNote: All 51 SRs paraphrased from IEC 62443-3-3:2013. Obtain normative text from iec.ch. SL descriptions represent the incremental requirements at each level; each higher SL includes all lower SL requirements.

requirements.name: Human user identification and authentication
requirements.description: Every human user accessing the IACS must be uniquely identified and authenticated before access is granted.
requirements.sl1: Unique user IDs and basic password authentication for all human user access to IACS components.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus enforce password complexity and minimum length; enforce account lockout after configurable failed attempts.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all human users accessing IACS components.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus hardware-based authentication tokens or biometrics; continuous authentication session monitoring.
requirements.otConsiderations: Legacy HMIs and engineering workstations may not support MFA natively. Use jump servers or remote desktop gateways as the MFA enforcement point. Shared accounts on PLCs should be eliminated at the zone boundary via privileged access management (PAM) tools.

requirements.name: Software process and device identification and authentication
requirements.description: Software processes and devices (PLCs, RTUs, sensors, historians) that communicate with other IACS components must be identified and authenticated.
requirements.sl1: Document and inventory all software processes and devices that communicate within the IACS.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus authenticate software processes and devices using at least shared-secret (e.g., pre-shared key) mechanisms.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus use PKI certificates or equivalent strong authentication for inter-device and inter-process communication.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus hardware-based device identity (TPM or equivalent); cryptographic attestation of device integrity at authentication.
requirements.otConsiderations: Most legacy OT devices have no native device authentication capability. Network-level controls (802.1X, MAC filtering, network access control) at zone boundaries are primary compensating controls. OPC-UA Security Mode Sign+Encrypt and DNP3 Secure Authentication v5 provide protocol-level device authentication for newer deployments.

requirements.name: Account management
requirements.description: IACS accounts must be managed throughout their lifecycle — creation, activation, modification, disabling, and removal.
requirements.sl1: Define and document account management procedures. Disable or remove accounts when no longer required.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus implement formal account provisioning and de-provisioning process with approvals. Review all active accounts at least annually.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus automated account lifecycle management; review privileged accounts quarterly; disable inactive accounts after defined period.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus continuous privileged access monitoring; session recording for all privileged access; just-in-time (JIT) privileged access.
requirements.otConsiderations: OT vendor accounts are frequently left active after commissioning or maintenance visits. Third-party and vendor accounts require particularly disciplined lifecycle management — disable immediately after maintenance windows close. Emergency/break-glass accounts must be tightly controlled and audited.

requirements.name: Identifier management
requirements.description: Identifiers (user IDs, device IDs, process IDs) used within the IACS must be managed to ensure uniqueness and prevent reuse of recently retired identifiers.
requirements.sl1: Ensure all user and device identifiers are unique within the IACS. Document identifier assignment.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus define and enforce identifier naming conventions; prevent immediate reuse of retired identifiers.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus minimum reuse delay for identifiers (e.g., 180 days); automated enforcement of identifier uniqueness.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus permanent retirement of identifiers associated with security incidents; cryptographically bound identifiers.
requirements.otConsiderations: Shared accounts ('operator', 'engineer', 'admin') are extremely common in OT and directly violate this requirement. Transitioning legacy OT environments to individual identifiers requires operational change management — operators resist change that might slow emergency response.

requirements.name: Authenticator management
requirements.description: Authenticators (passwords, tokens, certificates, keys) used in the IACS must be managed securely throughout their lifecycle.
requirements.sl1: Establish minimum password requirements. Change default passwords on all devices at commissioning. Protect authenticators from disclosure.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus enforce password expiry, history (prevent reuse), and secure storage (hashed/salted). Manage certificate lifecycles with renewal alerts.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus centralised credential vault for privileged credentials; hardware security modules (HSM) for critical cryptographic keys.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus HSM for all authentication credential storage; automated secret rotation; cryptographic key ceremony procedures.
requirements.otConsiderations: Default credentials on OT equipment (Siemens, Schneider, Rockwell, Honeywell, ABB) are extensively documented in public sources and actively exploited. Changing defaults at commissioning is the single highest-impact low-cost action. Many embedded devices store passwords in cleartext — compensate with network segmentation.

requirements.name: Wireless access management
requirements.description: Wireless access to the IACS must be managed, controlled, and monitored to prevent unauthorised access via wireless interfaces.
requirements.sl1: Document all wireless access points in the IACS network. Disable unnecessary wireless interfaces on IACS devices.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus use WPA3 or equivalent strong encryption for all IACS wireless networks. Authenticate all wireless clients.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus dedicated OT wireless networks separate from corporate WiFi; wireless IDS to detect rogue access points; MFA for wireless access.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus continuous wireless spectrum monitoring; automatic deauthentication of unauthorised devices; hardware-level disable of unused wireless interfaces.
requirements.otConsiderations: Industrial wireless (WirelessHART, ISA100.11a, Zigbee) is increasingly deployed for sensor networks. These protocols have different security profiles from standard WiFi and require dedicated assessment. Portable handheld devices used for engineering access via WiFi are a common attack vector.

requirements.name: Strength of password-based authentication
requirements.description: Where password-based authentication is used, passwords must meet minimum strength requirements proportional to the security level.
requirements.sl1: Minimum 8-character passwords with at least one character class restriction (uppercase, lowercase, numeric, or special).
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus minimum 12 characters, at least 3 character classes, enforced by system.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus minimum 15 characters, all 4 character classes, enforced complexity, no dictionary words.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus passwords as secondary factor only — primary authentication must be hardware token or biometric.
requirements.otConsiderations: Older OT HMIs may only support short passwords (4-8 characters) due to software limitations. Document these as exceptions with compensating controls (physical access restriction, jump server interposition). Do not bypass the requirement — document and mitigate.

requirements.name: Public key infrastructure certificates
requirements.description: Where PKI certificates are used for authentication, the IACS must include controls for certificate issuance, validation, revocation and renewal.
requirements.sl1: Not required at SL 1.
requirements.sl2: If PKI certificates are used, maintain a certificate inventory and track expiry dates.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus operate or connect to a dedicated OT PKI or certificate authority; enforce certificate validation and revocation checking.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus hardware-backed PKI with HSM; certificate pinning for critical communications; automated certificate lifecycle management.
requirements.otConsiderations: Certificate expiry has caused OT outages — expired TLS certificates blocking historian-to-ERP communications or OPC-UA connections. Certificate lifecycle management is frequently overlooked in OT. Industrial protocols increasingly use certificates (OPC-UA, MQTT-TLS, IEC 62351).

requirements.name: Strength of public key authentication
requirements.description: Where public key cryptography is used for authentication, the key strengths and algorithms must meet current standards for the required security level.
requirements.sl1: Not required at SL 1.
requirements.sl2: Minimum RSA-2048 or ECC-256. Avoid deprecated algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4).
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus RSA-3072 or ECC-384 minimum; algorithm agility to allow migration.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus post-quantum cryptography readiness assessment; hybrid classical/PQC schemes where available.
requirements.otConsiderations: Many OT components have constrained processors that struggle with RSA-2048 operations at scale. ECC provides equivalent security with lower computational overhead — prefer ECC for resource-constrained OT devices.

requirements.name: Authenticator feedback
requirements.description: The IACS must not provide information that would assist an attacker in determining valid credentials — authentication failure messages must not distinguish between invalid username and invalid password.
requirements.sl1: Authentication failure messages must not reveal whether the username or password was incorrect.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus implement consistent response time for successful and failed authentication (prevent timing attacks).
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus log all authentication failures with timestamp, source, and account attempted.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus real-time alerting on authentication anomalies; automatic blocking of sources with abnormal authentication patterns.
requirements.otConsiderations: SCADA web interfaces are frequently vulnerable to user enumeration through different error messages for valid vs invalid usernames. Test all web-based IACS interfaces for this vulnerability during security assessments.

requirements.name: Unsuccessful login attempts
requirements.description: The IACS must enforce limits on consecutive unsuccessful authentication attempts and take defined action when limits are exceeded.
requirements.sl1: Define and document policy for handling unsuccessful login attempts. Implement account lockout or increasing delay.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus enforce account lockout after 5 or fewer consecutive failed attempts with minimum 15-minute lockout or manual administrator unlock.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus automatic alerting to security operations on lockout events; lockout for network-facing interfaces.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus lockout triggers block at network level (firewall rule) for source IP; automatic incident ticket generation.
requirements.otConsiderations: Account lockout in OT environments requires careful configuration — locking the single operator account at a remote site during an emergency could be catastrophic. Implement lockout with operational bypass procedures (break-glass) and ensure the lockout policy is tested in non-production conditions.

requirements.name: System use notification
requirements.description: The IACS must display an approved system use notification (warning banner) to users before granting access, informing them of authorised use, monitoring, and consequences of unauthorised use.
requirements.sl1: Display a system use notification banner on all IACS interfaces before authentication.
requirements.sl2: SL1 — no additional requirement at SL 2.
requirements.sl3: SL2 — no additional requirement at SL 3.
requirements.sl4: SL3 — no additional requirement at SL 4.
requirements.otConsiderations: Banners serve a dual legal and psychological deterrence function. In Malaysian law, a login banner that warns of monitoring and states that use is restricted to authorised personnel supports prosecution under the Computer Crimes Act 1997 (Act 563). Ensure banners are reviewed by legal counsel.

requirements.name: Access via untrusted networks
requirements.description: All access to the IACS via untrusted networks (internet, corporate IT network, third-party networks) must be explicitly controlled and authenticated through a defined access point.
requirements.sl1: Identify and document all paths by which the IACS can be accessed via untrusted networks. Require authentication for all such paths.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus implement VPN or equivalent encrypted tunnel for all remote access; log all remote access sessions.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus MFA required for all remote access; dedicated jump server or bastion host as the sole remote access path; record and monitor all remote sessions.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus vendor remote access requires separate approval per session with time-limited credentials; all sessions recorded with operator presence required; zero-trust architecture for remote access.
requirements.otConsiderations: Remote access for vendor maintenance and support is the most common initial access vector in OT incidents. Solutions like Claroty xDome Secure Access, Fortinet FortiSIEM, Tosibox, and PAS OT:CERT provide OT-specific secure remote access. Never allow direct RDP or VNC from the internet to OT assets. COVID-19 accelerated OT remote access adoption dramatically — many sites have lingering insecure remote access configurations.

requirements.name: Authorization enforcement
requirements.description: The IACS must enforce authorisation decisions for all authenticated users and processes — limiting access to only the resources and functions explicitly granted.
requirements.sl1: Implement role-based or attribute-based access control. Users must only access resources their role requires.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus enforce least privilege; define and document roles with associated permissions; audit privilege assignments.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus separation of duties for critical functions; no single user can perform high-impact actions without dual authorisation.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus just-in-time privilege elevation with approval workflow and automatic expiry; all privilege use logged and reviewed.
requirements.otConsiderations: OT systems frequently use a flat permission model (read-only vs full control). Fine-grained RBAC must be implemented at the application layer (SCADA/DCS configuration) rather than relying on OS-level permissions, as OT application RBAC may not map to OS users.

requirements.name: Wireless use control
requirements.description: Wireless usage within the IACS must be authorised, controlled, and monitored. Unauthorised wireless devices must be detected and blocked.
requirements.sl1: Authorise and document all wireless devices and access points. Prohibit use of unauthorised wireless devices.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus actively detect unauthorised wireless devices on the OT network; monitor wireless traffic.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus automatic blocking or isolation of unauthorised wireless devices; periodic wireless survey.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus continuous wireless spectrum monitoring with automated deauthentication of unauthorised devices.
requirements.otConsiderations: Engineers frequently bring personal WiFi hotspots or Bluetooth-enabled laptops into OT environments for convenience. Portable handheld devices (tablets, phones) with cellular connectivity represent a bypass of network segmentation if connected to OT assets. Strict device management policies and physical entry controls are required.

requirements.name: Use of portable and mobile devices
requirements.description: The use of portable storage media and mobile devices within the IACS must be controlled to prevent introduction of malware and unauthorised data exfiltration.
requirements.sl1: Define and document policy for portable and mobile device use in OT. Restrict USB to authorised devices.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus technically enforce USB restrictions; scan portable media before use in OT; maintain inventory of authorised devices.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus dedicated OT-only scanning workstations for media; mobile device management (MDM) for authorised portable devices in OT.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus disable all removable media interfaces on OT assets unless specifically required; approve each use individually.
requirements.otConsiderations: Stuxnet propagated via USB — this attack vector remains active and relevant. Engineers routinely transfer patches, configurations, and historian exports via USB drives. OT-specific data diode kiosks (e.g., Honeywell Secure Media Exchange, Nexus Controls) provide a secure transfer mechanism. Train all OT personnel on USB security.

requirements.name: Mobile code
requirements.description: Mobile code (scripts, macros, applets, ActiveX controls) executed within the IACS must be controlled to prevent execution of unauthorised or malicious code.
requirements.sl1: Define policy on acceptable mobile code in OT. Disable browser plugins and scripting on OT workstations where not operationally required.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus whitelist permitted mobile code and block all others; disable PowerShell, VBScript, JavaScript where not required on OT.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus application whitelisting enforces code execution policy; signed mobile code required; code review for any scripts used in OT.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus no mobile code execution on safety-critical systems; static analysis of all code before introduction to OT.
requirements.otConsiderations: SCADA HMIs frequently run on Windows with full browser and scripting capability. Disabling unnecessary scripting on HMIs is low-cost and eliminates a significant attack surface. Application whitelisting tools (Carbon Black, Ivanti, Microsoft AppLocker) are widely used in OT environments.

requirements.name: Session lock
requirements.description: The IACS must implement session lock after a defined period of inactivity, requiring re-authentication to resume the session.
requirements.sl1: Implement session lock or screen lock after inactivity for all IACS user sessions.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus maximum inactivity timeout of 15 minutes for standard users; automatic lock enforced.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus inactivity timeout of 5 minutes for privileged sessions; lock events logged.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus presence detection to lock immediately on user departure from workstation.
requirements.otConsiderations: Session lock timeouts must be carefully calibrated in OT — an operator absent during a critical process event who returns to a locked screen could delay response. Shorter timeouts for engineering workstations and longer timeouts (with additional physical controls) for operator HMIs during active operations is a reasonable compromise.

requirements.name: Remote session termination
requirements.description: The IACS must provide the capability to terminate remote access sessions — both by the user and by an administrator.
requirements.sl1: Remote access sessions can be terminated by the user. Idle sessions time out automatically.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus administrators can terminate any active remote session; maximum session duration limits enforced.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus forced re-authentication after session duration limit; remote session termination triggers alert.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus operations team can terminate remote vendor sessions with single action; all session terminations logged.
requirements.otConsiderations: Vendor maintenance sessions that outlast the maintenance window (vendor forgets to disconnect) are a common gap. Session duration limits with mandatory operator acknowledgement before extension provide an effective control.

requirements.name: Concurrent session control
requirements.description: The IACS must control the number of concurrent sessions for each user account or account type to prevent account sharing and unauthorised access.
requirements.sl1: Define and document policy on concurrent session limits.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus technically enforce concurrent session limits per user; alert on concurrent session attempts.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus maximum one concurrent session for privileged accounts; second login attempt terminates first session or is denied.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus all concurrent session attempts generate security alert and are investigated.
requirements.otConsiderations: Concurrent session controls help detect credential sharing and compromised accounts. In OT shift handover scenarios, ensure the control is configured to allow clean session transfer between operators rather than blocking legitimate handover.

requirements.name: Auditable events
requirements.description: The IACS must generate audit records for security-relevant events — including authentication, authorisation, configuration changes, and system errors.
requirements.sl1: Generate audit records for login/logout, authentication failures, and system errors.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus audit records for privilege use, configuration changes, account management actions, and remote access. Forward logs to a centralised log management system.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus audit records for all administrative actions, policy changes, network connection events. Real-time log forwarding to SIEM/SOC.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus immutable audit logging; tamper-evident log storage; audit of all operator actions at safety-critical consoles.
requirements.otConsiderations: OT devices generate logs in vendor-specific formats (GE Historian events, Siemens SIMATIC logs, OSIsoft PI event frames). Log normalisation and forwarding to a SIEM (Splunk, QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel) requires OT-specific log connectors or a purpose-built OT security platform (Dragos, Claroty, Nozomi). Passive log collection (syslog, WMI) must not disrupt control system timing.

requirements.name: Audit storage capacity
requirements.description: The IACS must allocate sufficient storage for audit records and take defined action when storage capacity approaches or reaches its limit.
requirements.sl1: Allocate sufficient log storage. Define action when storage limit is reached (overwrite oldest, alert, shutdown).
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus alert when log storage reaches 80% capacity; minimum 90-day local log retention.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus minimum 1-year log retention in centralised storage; automated capacity monitoring with escalation.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus minimum 7-year log retention for incident investigation; immutable write-once storage.
requirements.otConsiderations: Many OT components have severely constrained local storage (embedded PLCs, RTUs). Log forwarding to a centralised syslog server or SIEM eliminates local storage constraints. For disconnected or intermittently connected sites, local buffering with store-and-forward to central collection is needed.

requirements.name: Response to audit processing failures
requirements.description: The IACS must alert authorised personnel and take defined actions when audit log generation or forwarding fails.
requirements.sl1: Alert when audit log generation fails. Define response procedure.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus automatic alert to security personnel on audit processing failure; document expected response.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus trigger security investigation on audit failure; evaluate whether failure is caused by attack (e.g., log deletion).
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus treat audit processing failure as a security incident; automatic escalation and increased monitoring.
requirements.otConsiderations: Log deletion or disabling is a tactic used by attackers to cover their tracks (MITRE ATT&CK for ICS T0872 — Indicator Removal on Host). Audit processing failures should be treated as security events requiring investigation, not routine maintenance issues.

requirements.name: Timestamps
requirements.description: The IACS must generate audit records with accurate timestamps using a reliable time source synchronised across all components.
requirements.sl1: All IACS components use a consistent time source for log timestamps.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus synchronise all IACS components to NTP or IEEE 1588 Precision Time Protocol; document time sources.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus use authenticated NTP (NTPsec or GPD with GPS clock); maximum time drift of 1 second; alert on time synchronisation failure.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus GPS-disciplined time source; cryptographically authenticated timestamps; time integrity monitoring.
requirements.otConsiderations: Accurate timestamps are critical for incident investigation and forensic correlation across OT layers (field device events, HMI operator actions, historian records, network logs). OT environments frequently have time drift due to isolated networks. IEEE 1588 PTP provides sub-microsecond precision for process control applications that require it.

requirements.name: Non-repudiation
requirements.description: The IACS must provide the capability to determine which user performed which actions — preventing users from credibly denying their actions.
requirements.sl1: Individual user accounts used for all IACS access. No shared accounts.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus audit records link actions to individual authenticated identities; records protected from modification.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus session recording for all privileged access; digital signatures on critical configuration changes.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus legally-admissible audit trail with cryptographic integrity; operator acknowledgement (digital signature) required for safety-critical commands.
requirements.otConsiderations: Non-repudiation supports regulatory enforcement (NACSA Act 854 s26 incident reporting), insurance claims, and legal proceedings following OT incidents. Immutable audit trails are increasingly required by cyber insurance providers.

requirements.name: Communication integrity
requirements.description: The IACS must protect the integrity of transmitted information — ensuring data has not been modified in transit between components.
requirements.sl1: Use network protocols that include integrity checking (e.g., TCP checksums). Document communications that lack integrity protection.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus apply message authentication codes (MAC) or digital signatures for inter-zone communications. Use integrity-supporting OT protocol variants (DNP3 SA, OPC-UA with signing).
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus cryptographic integrity protection for all inter-zone communications; detect and alert on integrity violations.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus hardware-enforced integrity at safety system boundaries; formal verification of safety-critical communication sequences.
requirements.otConsiderations: Legacy OT protocols (Modbus, classic DNP3, older PROFINET) have no native integrity protection — anyone on the OT network segment can replay or modify commands. Network segmentation provides partial mitigation but does not address insider threats or compromised OT workstations within the segment.

requirements.name: Malicious code protection
requirements.description: The IACS must protect against malicious code (malware) including viruses, worms, Trojans, ransomware, and spyware.
requirements.sl1: Where supported, deploy antivirus/antimalware on OT workstations with regular signature updates.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus application whitelisting on OT workstations and servers where antivirus is insufficient; scan all external media before introduction.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus network-level malware detection (IDS with OT signature sets); behavioural anomaly detection for OT environments.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus formal malware analysis capability for OT-specific malware; threat intelligence integration for OT malware indicators.
requirements.otConsiderations: Traditional antivirus cannot be run on most PLCs and embedded controllers. For OT workstations running Windows, antivirus with OT-vendor-approved signature sets (Siemens, Rockwell, GE approve specific AV products) is the baseline. Application whitelisting (Tripwire, Carbon Black, Ivanti Application Control) is preferred in OT as it does not require signature updates that could cause conflicts.

requirements.name: Security functionality verification
requirements.description: The IACS must provide mechanisms to verify that security functions operate correctly — including the ability to run self-tests and integrity checks.
requirements.sl1: Document and periodically test security functions (authentication, access control, logging).
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus scheduled automated testing of security controls; alert on control failure.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus continuous automated verification of security control effectiveness; security function failures generate incident.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus formal security function verification as part of operational safety testing; periodic red team exercises.
requirements.otConsiderations: Security function verification in OT must be planned during maintenance windows to avoid disrupting operational processes. Passive verification methods (log review, configuration audit) are preferred over active testing that could affect OT behaviour.

requirements.name: Software and information integrity
requirements.description: The IACS must protect the integrity of software and stored information using cryptographic mechanisms or equivalent controls.
requirements.sl1: Maintain an inventory of all software (firmware, OS, applications) deployed on OT components with version information.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus use cryptographic hash verification for software and firmware before installation; verify integrity of OT backups.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus continuous file integrity monitoring (FIM) on OT workstations and servers; detect unauthorised changes.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus hardware-based secure boot for all OT components that support it; firmware integrity verification at startup.
requirements.otConsiderations: PLC firmware modification is an advanced attack technique (Stuxnet modified Siemens S7 firmware). Firmware integrity verification during maintenance is critical. For older PLCs without integrity checking, verify firmware checksums manually against vendor-published values before every upload.

requirements.name: Input validation
requirements.description: The IACS must validate all inputs to protect against malformed data that could cause unexpected behaviour, crashes, or exploitation.
requirements.sl1: Validate critical process inputs at the application level. Document input ranges for key process variables.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus validate all external inputs (operator commands, network data, file imports); reject or sanitise invalid inputs.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus input validation at every trust boundary; alert on input validation failures.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus formal verification of input validation logic for safety-critical inputs; hardware interlocks for safety-critical setpoint limits.
requirements.otConsiderations: Process value manipulation (injecting false sensor readings into a SCADA system to hide true process state) is a sophisticated attack technique. Both software input validation and independent physical measurement verification (cross-check between independent sensor channels) are needed for safety-critical inputs.

requirements.name: Deterministic output
requirements.description: The IACS must produce deterministic, expected outputs when given valid inputs — and handle invalid or unexpected inputs without entering unsafe states.
requirements.sl1: Define expected outputs for normal operating conditions. Test system response to out-of-range inputs.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus define and test fail-safe states for all credible input faults.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus formal analysis of failure modes; safety system acts as backstop for process control failures.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus formal verification of deterministic output requirements for safety-critical functions; independent safety validation.
requirements.otConsiderations: This requirement overlaps with IEC 61511 (Functional Safety) for safety instrumented systems. For SIS, deterministic safe-state output under all failure modes — including cyber-induced failures — must be demonstrated. The TRITON attack targeted exactly this: manipulating the SIS to prevent it from triggering a safe shutdown.

requirements.name: Error handling
requirements.description: The IACS must handle errors in a secure manner — not revealing sensitive system information in error messages and failing to a defined safe state.
requirements.sl1: Error messages do not reveal system configuration, software version, or internal state to unauthorised users.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus define safe states for all credible error conditions; test error handling paths.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus errors that may indicate attack are escalated to security operations; diagnostic information only available to authenticated administrators.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus all errors in safety-critical components generate safety system alert; formal error state analysis.
requirements.otConsiderations: SCADA and HMI systems frequently display verbose error messages that reveal software versions, database names, and connection strings. These are reconnaissance goldmines for attackers. Review all user-facing error messages and ensure diagnostic detail is logged internally only.

requirements.name: Session integrity
requirements.description: The IACS must protect communication sessions against hijacking, replay attacks, and man-in-the-middle attacks.
requirements.sl1: Use session management mechanisms that prevent trivial session hijacking (unique session tokens, session invalidation on logout).
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus cryptographically protected sessions (TLS 1.2+ for web-based SCADA); replay protection for control commands.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus session integrity verification throughout the session lifetime; detect and alert on anomalous session behaviour.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus mutual authentication and session encryption for all safety-critical communications; hardware-enforced session security.
requirements.otConsiderations: OT protocol replay attacks are a real threat — replaying captured legitimate commands to a PLC can cause unintended process actions. DNP3 Secure Authentication and OPC-UA Security provide replay protection. For legacy protocols without native replay protection, network-level controls (sequence number monitoring, timing analysis) provide partial mitigation.

requirements.name: Protection of audit information
requirements.description: Audit records must be protected from unauthorised access, modification, and deletion — maintaining their integrity and availability for incident investigation.
requirements.sl1: Restrict write and delete access to audit records to authorised personnel only.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus audit records stored on dedicated log server separate from operational systems; regular backup of audit records.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus tamper-evident log storage with integrity verification; alert on any unauthorised modification attempt.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus immutable (write-once) audit log storage; cryptographic proof of log integrity; long-term archival with chain of custody.
requirements.otConsiderations: Log manipulation (deleting logs to cover tracks) is standard attacker behaviour. Centralised logging with write-only permissions for OT systems (syslog to a hardened log collector) is the key control. Ensure the log infrastructure itself is within scope of OT security controls.

requirements.name: Information confidentiality
requirements.description: The IACS must protect the confidentiality of information — process configurations, network topology, user credentials, and operational data — from unauthorised disclosure.
requirements.sl1: Identify and classify sensitive IACS information. Restrict access to sensitive information to authorised personnel.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus encrypt sensitive data at rest on OT workstations and servers; protect data in transit between zones with encryption.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus full-disk encryption on OT engineering workstations; data loss prevention (DLP) controls on OT-IT boundary.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus hardware-enforced data classification; cryptographic access control for sensitive configuration files.
requirements.otConsiderations: In OT, the most sensitive confidential information is often network topology diagrams, PLC program logic, and process configurations — not personal data. This information enables sophisticated attacks. Restrict distribution of network diagrams, PLC configurations, and safety system logic to need-to-know personnel.

requirements.name: Information persistence
requirements.description: The IACS must prevent sensitive information from persisting in locations where it could be accessed by unauthorised parties — including memory, temporary files, and decommissioned media.
requirements.sl1: Define media sanitisation procedures for OT equipment disposal and re-use.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus wipe sensitive data from OT media before disposal or reuse; clear swap files and temporary storage.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus cryptographic erasure for sensitive OT storage media; physical destruction for highest-sensitivity media.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus memory scrubbing for runtime protection; hardware-enforced data persistence prevention on safety system components.
requirements.otConsiderations: Decommissioned OT equipment (PLCs, HMIs, engineering laptops) frequently contains sensitive process configurations, credentials, and network information. Proper media sanitisation before disposal or transfer is consistently overlooked in OT environments.

requirements.name: Use of cryptography
requirements.description: Where cryptography is used in the IACS, it must employ approved algorithms, adequate key lengths, and proper key management practices.
requirements.sl1: Avoid deprecated cryptographic algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, DES, 3DES, RC4). Document cryptographic usage.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus use current strong algorithms (AES-256, SHA-256+, RSA-2048+, ECC-256+). Manage encryption keys with defined procedures.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus hardware security modules (HSM) for key storage; cryptographic algorithm inventory with migration plan.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus post-quantum cryptography readiness; algorithm agility to migrate without service disruption.
requirements.otConsiderations: Many OT systems use embedded cryptographic libraries that are years behind current standards and cannot be updated without vendor support. The cryptographic inventory (which algorithms, where, on what devices) is the first step — create this before attempting remediation. For legacy devices that cannot use current algorithms, network-level encryption (VPN tunnels) provides compensating protection.

requirements.name: Network segmentation
requirements.description: The IACS must be segmented from other networks (IT networks, internet, third-party networks) using defined zones and conduits with access controls at zone boundaries.
requirements.sl1: Physically or logically separate IACS from corporate IT networks and the internet. Document network boundaries.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus implement firewall rules at all zone boundaries; permit only required traffic; deny by default. Implement Industrial DMZ (IDMZ) between IT and OT.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus deep packet inspection (DPI) with OT protocol-aware rules at zone boundaries; IDS at conduits; unidirectional gateways for highest-risk conduits.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus hardware-enforced unidirectional data flows (data diodes) for safety system communications; physical network separation for SIS.
requirements.otConsiderations: The Industrial DMZ (IDMZ) is the foundational OT security architecture. It breaks the network path between Level 4 (IT) and Level 3 (OT) and forces all data exchange through controlled services (historian replication, file transfer, patch servers). Air-gapping safety systems from DCS is the SL 4 standard for SIS protection.

requirements.name: Zone boundary protection
requirements.description: The IACS must protect zone boundaries by monitoring and controlling all communications that cross between zones — enforcing zone security policies at every boundary crossing.
requirements.sl1: All traffic crossing zone boundaries passes through a security control (firewall, managed switch with ACLs).
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus stateful inspection at all zone boundaries; deny all traffic not explicitly permitted; document all permitted traffic flows.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus application-layer filtering at zone boundaries; OT-protocol-aware firewalling; IDS at all zone boundaries; alert on boundary violation attempts.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus hardware data diodes at safety system zone boundaries; formal traffic flow authorisation process with approval records.
requirements.otConsiderations: Zone boundary protection requires a current and accurate network topology model. Many OT environments have undocumented connections (temporary laptop connections, vendor modems, remote I/O over public network) that bypass intended zone boundaries. Network discovery and topology mapping is a prerequisite.

requirements.name: General purpose person-to-person communication restrictions
requirements.description: General-purpose person-to-person communication channels (email, instant messaging, social media, VoIP) must be restricted from IACS components to the minimum operationally necessary.
requirements.sl1: Define and document which communication channels are permitted on OT networks.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus block email clients, general internet browsing, and social media on OT workstations at network level.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus whitelist permitted outbound communication protocols from OT; block all others.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus no person-to-person communication capability on safety system components.
requirements.otConsiderations: Email on OT workstations is a significant malware delivery vector — phishing attacks that succeed on corporate email systems can reach OT if email clients are available on SCADA workstations. Block email clients on all OT workstations and provide a separate IT-side terminal for engineers who need email access.

requirements.name: Application partitioning
requirements.description: IACS applications must be partitioned to separate user-facing interfaces from underlying control functions — preventing compromise of one from directly compromising the other.
requirements.sl1: Separate operator HMI from direct PLC programming access. Document application trust relationships.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus separate engineering functions (PLC programming) from operations functions (process monitoring and control) at user account and system level.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus dedicated engineering workstations separate from operator HMIs; separate networks for engineering access.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus hardware-enforced separation of safety system logic from process control logic; formal verification of partitioning.
requirements.otConsiderations: Combining operator HMI and engineering workstation functions on the same device is common in small OT sites and creates significant risk — a compromised operator workstation gains PLC programming access. Separate physical or virtual machines for engineering functions is the baseline SL 2 architecture.

requirements.name: Audit log accessibility
requirements.description: Audit logs must be accessible to authorised security personnel for review, analysis, and incident investigation in a timely manner.
requirements.sl1: Audit logs are accessible to administrators for manual review.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus centralised log management with search capability; logs accessible within 24 hours of request.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus real-time log access for security operations; SIEM integration with OT log sources; correlation between OT and IT events.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus dedicated OT security monitoring platform (Dragos, Claroty, Nozomi or equivalent) with real-time OT event visibility; forensic-quality log preservation.
requirements.otConsiderations: NACSA Act 854 s26 requires notification of significant cybersecurity incidents within 6 hours. Accessible, correlated audit logs are essential to determine whether an event meets the notification threshold within the required timeframe. Without accessible OT logs, operators cannot make timely incident determinations.

requirements.name: Continuous monitoring
requirements.description: The IACS must provide continuous monitoring capability to detect security events and anomalies in real time or near-real time.
requirements.sl1: Perform periodic security monitoring — at minimum, review logs weekly for anomalies.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus automated monitoring with alerting for defined security events; monitoring during all operational hours.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus 24/7 security monitoring with OT-specific detection rules; anomaly detection using baseline behavioural models; dedicated OT SOC capability or managed OT security service.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus safety system monitoring with automatic alert on any anomalous command to safety functions; integration with operational safety systems.
requirements.otConsiderations: Passive network monitoring is the standard approach in OT — tools like Dragos Platform, Claroty Continuous Threat Detection, Nozomi Networks, and Microsoft Defender for IoT perform deep packet inspection of OT protocols (Modbus, DNP3, EtherNet/IP, PROFINET) without sending any packets that could disrupt control systems. Asset discovery and vulnerability identification are additional benefits.

requirements.name: Denial of service protection
requirements.description: The IACS must protect against denial-of-service attacks that could impair availability of critical control functions.
requirements.sl1: Configure network devices to rate-limit broadcast traffic. Disable unused network services and ports on OT devices.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus implement traffic shaping and QoS to prioritise control traffic; network-level DoS protection at zone boundaries.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus resilient communications paths (redundant network interfaces, multiple paths); OT-aware DoS detection.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus hardware-enforced traffic prioritisation for safety-critical communications; automatic isolation of affected segments on DoS detection.
requirements.otConsiderations: OT systems are particularly vulnerable to DoS — sending a flood of valid Modbus requests to a PLC can overwhelm its processing capacity and prevent it from executing control logic. Network segmentation and rate limiting at zone boundaries are the primary defences. Redundant communication paths provide resilience against both network failures and DoS.

requirements.name: Resource management
requirements.description: The IACS must manage computing and network resources to ensure availability of critical functions — preventing resource exhaustion from disrupting process control.
requirements.sl1: Monitor CPU, memory, and network utilisation on OT components. Define thresholds for alert.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus automated resource monitoring with alerting; resource reservations for critical control functions.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus resource quotas preventing non-critical processes from consuming resources needed for control; capacity planning reviews.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus hardware resource isolation for safety-critical functions; formal capacity analysis for all OT components.
requirements.otConsiderations: PLCs and embedded controllers have limited CPU and memory. Installing unauthorised software on OT workstations can consume resources needed for HMI responsiveness or historian archiving. Baseline resource utilisation measurements and monitor for unexpected increases (which may indicate malware or misconfiguration).

requirements.name: Control system backup
requirements.description: The IACS must maintain current, tested backups of control system configurations, programs, and data to enable timely recovery after a cyber incident or failure.
requirements.sl1: Back up PLC programs, HMI configurations, historian data, and SCADA databases. Store backups offline.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus automated backups with defined schedule; verify backup integrity; store copies offsite or on isolated media.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus test backup restoration quarterly; include backup procedures in incident response plan; immutable backup storage.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus real-time mirroring for critical control configurations; rapid restoration capability tested under simulated cyber incident conditions.
requirements.otConsiderations: OT backup is frequently overlooked because equipment has been running without change for years. When a PLC program needs to be restored after a wiper malware attack, discovering that the last backup was from years ago — or doesn't exist — is catastrophic. Include all OT components in backup scope: PLC ladder logic, HMI screens and tags, SCADA database, historian configuration, network device configs.

requirements.name: Control system recovery and reconstitution
requirements.description: The IACS must have documented and tested procedures for recovering control system functions to a known state after a cyber incident.
requirements.sl1: Document recovery procedures for each critical OT system. Include vendor contact information and spare equipment locations.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus test recovery procedures annually; define recovery time objectives (RTO) per system; train operations and engineering staff.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus cyber incident-specific recovery procedures; integrate OT recovery with IT BC/DR plans; test OT recovery under realistic simulated cyber incident conditions.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus maintain warm standby for critical control functions; recovery procedures include safety team coordination protocols; formal post-incident review process.
requirements.otConsiderations: OT recovery after a cyber incident is fundamentally different from IT recovery — it involves physical process reconstitution, not just data restoration. A recovered DCS that is placed back in control of a running process must be synchronised with actual plant state to avoid process upsets. OT recovery plans must include operations engineering coordination, not just IT restoration steps.

requirements.name: Emergency power
requirements.description: Critical IACS components must have emergency power supply (UPS, generator) to maintain operation during power failures — preventing loss of process control from power interruption.
requirements.sl1: Identify OT components requiring continuous power for safety or operational continuity. Document power supply arrangements.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus UPS for critical OT components with sufficient capacity to enable safe process shutdown.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus generator backup for extended outages; UPS and generator tested regularly; include power in OT risk assessment.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus redundant power feeds from separate sources; automatic power transfer with no loss of control; power system resilience matches safety system SIL requirements.
requirements.otConsiderations: Power interruptions to OT systems are both a physical safety risk and a cyber resilience concern — attackers who compromise power management systems (as in Ukraine 2015) can use power disruption as a force multiplier. Treat OT power systems as within scope of OT security assessments.

requirements.name: Network and security configuration settings
requirements.description: Network and security configuration settings for IACS components must be documented, secured, and maintained — preventing unauthorised changes to security-relevant configuration.
requirements.sl1: Document baseline network and security configurations for all OT components. Review configurations annually.
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus configuration version control; alert on unauthorised configuration changes; restore to baseline after changes.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus continuous configuration monitoring with automated comparison against approved baseline; all changes require change management approval.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus cryptographically signed configurations; hardware-enforced configuration integrity; immutable configuration for safety systems.
requirements.otConsiderations: Configuration drift (unauthorised or undocumented changes accumulating over time) is common in OT environments. Tools like Tripwire, Tofino Xenon, and OT-specific asset management solutions provide automated configuration baseline monitoring. Configuration backup (SR 7.3) and configuration monitoring are complementary controls.

requirements.name: Least functionality
requirements.description: The IACS must be configured to provide only the functions, protocols, and services necessary for its operational purpose — disabling all unnecessary functionality.
requirements.sl1: Disable unnecessary services and protocols on OT components (telnet, FTP, HTTP if HTTPS available, unused COM ports).
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus document all enabled services and protocols with justification; periodic review to remove unnecessary capabilities.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus application whitelisting enforces least functionality; automated detection of new or changed services.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus formal approval required for any new service or protocol; safety systems have no functionality beyond their defined safety function.
requirements.otConsiderations: Legacy OT equipment is often shipped with unnecessary services enabled (telnet, web server for HMI, TFTP, SNMP v1/v2). Disabling telnet and replacing with SSH where available is a high-priority action. Some services cannot be disabled without affecting vendor support — document these as accepted exceptions with compensating controls.

requirements.name: Control system component inventory
requirements.description: The IACS must maintain a current inventory of all hardware and software components — the foundation of all other security controls.
requirements.sl1: Maintain an inventory of all IACS hardware components (make, model, firmware version, IP address, physical location).
requirements.sl2: SL1 plus include software (OS version, application version, patch level) in inventory; update within 5 days of changes; include all network-connected devices.
requirements.sl3: SL2 plus automated asset discovery and inventory maintenance using passive network monitoring; alert on new or changed devices.
requirements.sl4: SL3 plus hardware-level asset identity (serial number, RFID tag); continuous automated inventory reconciliation; alert on any unregistered device communicating on OT network.
requirements.otConsiderations: You cannot protect what you cannot see. An accurate OT asset inventory is the single most important foundational security activity. Passive network monitoring tools (Claroty, Dragos, Nozomi) provide automated OT asset discovery without requiring active scanning that could disrupt control systems. The inventory must include: PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, engineering workstations, historians, switches, routers, serial-to-Ethernet converters, and any device communicating on the OT network.
